message:
feat(fsqlite-core): add trigger definition storage and snapshot/restore support

Introduces in-memory trigger storage infrastructure for Phase 5G.2/5G.3
trigger execution support:

- Add TriggerDef struct to capture CREATE TRIGGER statement metadata:
  name, table_name, timing (BEFORE/AFTER/INSTEAD OF), event (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE),
  for_each_row flag, WHEN clause, body statements, temporary flag, and raw SQL
- Add TriggerDef::from_create_statement() constructor for parsing
- Add triggers: RefCell<Vec<TriggerDef>> field to Connection
- Include triggers in DbSnapshot for savepoint/rollback support
- Update restore_snapshot() to restore trigger state on rollback
- Update Connection::vacuum() to clear triggers on VACUUM
- Extend sqlite_master import logic to parse and store trigger definitions
  alongside tables, enabling trigger persistence across re-open

The actual trigger execution during DML will be implemented in Phase 5G.2/5G.3.

Also syncs beads issue tracker with development state.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-mxx9","title":"5D.5: Verify WAL checkpoint writes pages back to database file","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:49:53.678062007Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:12:57.009984904Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T06:12:57.009964636Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-mxx9","depends_on_id":"bd-ujkm","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:50:08.186842732Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1263,"issue_id":"bd-mxx9","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# 5D.5: Verify WAL checkpoint writes pages back to database file

## What

WAL mode defers writing pages to the main database file. Over time, the WAL file grows.
Checkpointing copies committed pages from the WAL back to the main .db file and resets
the WAL.

This task verifies that the existing checkpoint implementation works correctly when the
full storage stack is wired (rather than just in unit tests).

## Checkpoint modes

- PASSIVE: Copy pages while not blocking readers or writers
- FULL: Wait for readers, copy all pages, block writers during copy
- RESTART: Like FULL, then reset WAL to beginning
- TRUNCATE: Like RESTART, then truncate WAL to zero bytes

## What to verify

1. After checkpoint, the main .db file contains all committed pages
2. After checkpoint + WAL truncate, the .db file is self-contained
3. The .db file after checkpoint is readable by C SQLite (rusqlite)
4. The WAL index is rebuilt correctly after checkpoint
5. Auto-checkpoint triggers correctly (default: every 1000 WAL frames)

## Integration test

```rust
#[test]
fn test_checkpoint_creates_valid_db_file() {
    let conn = Connection::open("test.db")?;
    conn.execute("CREATE TABLE t(x INTEGER)")?;
    for i in 0..100 {
        conn.execute_with_params("INSERT INTO t VALUES (?)", &[SqliteValue::Integer(i)])?;
    }

    // Force checkpoint
    conn.execute("PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)")?;

    // Verify .db file is self-contained and valid
    // Open with rusqlite and verify all 100 rows
    let c_conn = rusqlite::Connection::open("test.db")?;
    let count: i64 = c_conn.query_row("SELECT count(*) FROM t", [], |r| r.get(0))?;
    assert_eq!(count, 100);
}
```

## Tests

- `test_passive_checkpoint`: INSERT many, passive checkpoint, verify .db updated
- `test_truncate_checkpoint`: Checkpoint TRUNCATE, verify WAL file gone/empty
- `test_auto_checkpoint_triggers`: Insert enough rows to trigger auto-checkpoint
- `test_checkpoint_respects_active_readers`: Reader holds snapshot, checkpoint skips those pages

 {"id":"bd-n3p4","title":"Corruption Models: Define realistic corruption modes + safety rails","description":"## Goal
Define the corruption modes we will simulate and how to keep them safe/reproducible.

## Corruption Modes (Initial)
- **Bit flips**: flip N random bits/bytes (seeded).
- **Page zeroing**: overwrite a full page-aligned 4KB chunk with zeros.
- **Truncation**: truncate file by M bytes / to page boundary.
- **WAL tail loss** (if WAL present): truncate `-wal` after K frames.

## Safety Rails
- Operate only on `work/` copies.
- Always record seed + offsets mutated.
- Keep pre-corruption copy to compare.

## Acceptance
- Documented corruption spec and how each will be detected (checksums + sqlite integrity_check expectations).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:09.929714225Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T04:40:53.264120210Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T04:40:53.264100463Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of existing E2E suite tasks; superseded by canonical bd-z0xm subgraph.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["durability","e2e","repair"],"comments":[{"id":1120,"issue_id":"bd-n3p4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria

 {"id":"bd-numl","title":"§4.19.1-4.19.5 Remote Effects Contract: RemoteCap + Named Computations + Leases + Idempotency + Sagas","description":"Implement §4.19.1-§4.19.5 remote effects contract: explicit RemoteCap, named computations (no closure shipping), lease-backed liveness, idempotency keys + dedup, and saga discipline for multi-step remote workflows.

## UNIT TEST REQUIREMENTS
- test_remote_cap_required_for_network_io: Attempt network I/O without RemoteCap in Cx; verify compile-time or runtime refusal with explicit error
- test_remote_cap_omitted_in_lab: Create LabRuntime Cx without RemoteCap; verify remote-dependent code paths fail gracefully (testable without network)
- test_named_computation_no_closure_shipping: Verify remote work is dispatched via ComputationName + serialized input bytes; verify arbitrary closures cannot be serialized/sent
- test_named_computation_registry: Register the four normative computation names (symbol_get_range, symbol_put_batch, segment_put, segment_stat); verify unregistered names are rejected
- test_lease_backed_liveness_expiry: Create a remote handle with a 100ms lease; wait 200ms without renewal; verify lease expiry triggers escalation (cancel/retry/fail) and is trace-visible
- test_idempotency_key_deterministic: Compute IdempotencyKey for same request bytes twice; verify identical keys (Trunc128(BLAKE3("fsqlite:remote:v1" || request_bytes)))
- test_idempotency_dedup_same_key_same_input: Submit same request twice with same IdempotencyKey; verify second returns recorded outcome (no double-write)
- test_idempotency_conflict_same_key_different_input: Submit two different computations with same IdempotencyKey; verify second is rejected as conflict

## E2E TEST
test_e2e_remote_effects_saga_eviction.rs: Perform an L2-to-L3 segment eviction as a Saga (upload -> verify -> retire local); cancel mid-saga after upload but before local retire; verify compensations run (uploaded segment is either cleaned up or local copy retained); restart the saga and verify it completes idempotently with no duplicate uploads.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] No network I/O occurs anywhere in FrankenSQLite without RemoteCap in the Cx capability set
- [ ] All remote operations use named computations (ComputationName enum), never serialized closures
- [ ] Lease expiry is always trace-visible and triggers deterministic escalation
- [ ] IdempotencyKey deduplication works correctly for retried requests and rejects conflicting requests
- [ ] Sagas for eviction and compaction either complete fully or compensate to a clean state","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:37:25.234174506Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T22:49:33.905311281Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T22:49:33.905289851Z","close_reason":"Enabled remote_effects module wiring in fsqlite-core and validated §4.19 RemoteCap/named-computation/idempotency/lease/saga test suite","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-numl","depends_on_id":"bd-3go","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:48:25.576559512Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-numl","depends_on_id":"bd-3go.12","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T07:32:08.196993719Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":169,"issue_id":"bd-numl","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# §4.19.1 Remote Named Computations + Idempotency + Sagas

## Spec Reference
COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENSQLITE_V1.md lines 5430–5510 (§4.19, §4.19.1–§4.19.5)

## Overview
Tiered storage (L3) and replication are fundamentally remote effects. FrankenSQLite
adopts asupersync's remote contract so remote behavior is cancellable, bounded, and
auditable. All remote operations must be named (no closure shipping), idempotent,
lease-backed, and saga-wrapped for multi-step workflows.

## Global Remote Bulkhead (Normative)
All remote operations (fetch, upload, anti-entropy RPCs) MUST run under a global
remote bulkhead with concurrency cap `PRAGMA fsqlite.remote_max_in_flight`
(0 = auto; §4.17.1). Prevents retry storms and kernel-level overload on many-core
machines when remote tiers degrade.

## §4.19.1 Explicit Remote Capability (Required)
- All remote operations MUST require `RemoteCap` in `Cx`.
- Without it: no network I/O (compile-time or runtime refusal).
- Native mode still functions under `durability = local`.
- Prevents silent network I/O from arbitrary SQL code paths.
- Lab contexts simply omit RemoteCap to test without network.

## §4.19.2 Named Computations (Required for Auditing)
Remote work MUST be specified by `ComputationName` + serialized input bytes.
Runtime MUST NEVER serialize arbitrary closures.

### Normative Remote Computation Names (Minimum Set)
1. `symbol_get_range(object_id, esi_lo, esi_hi, ecs_epoch)` — fetch range of symbols
2. `symbol_put_batch(object_id, symbols[], ecs_epoch)` — upload batch of symbols
3. `segment_put(segment_id, bytes, ecs_epoch)` — upload segment blob
4. `segment_stat(segment_id, ecs_epoch)` — stat a remote segment
5. No closure shipping: all are named + serialized inputs only.

## §4.19.3 Lease-Backed Liveness (Required)
- Remote handles MUST be lease-backed.
- If lease expires: local region MUST escalate (cancel, retry, or fail).
- Event MUST be trace-visible.
- Avoids "hung remote fetch" as unbounded tail-latency failure.

## §4.19.4 Idempotency (Required)
All remote requests that might be retried MUST include an IdempotencyKey:
```
IdempotencyKey = Trunc128(BLAKE3("fsqlite:remote:v1" || request_bytes))
```
Remote receivers MUST deduplicate by IdempotencyKey (asupersync IdempotencyStore):
- Duplicate with same computation name + inputs → return recorded outcome.
- Duplicate with same key but different computation inputs → conflict, MUST reject.

## §4.19.5 Sagas for Multi-Step Publication (Required)
Any multi-step remote+local workflow that would leave partial state on
cancellation/crash MUST be expressed as a Saga (forward steps + deterministic compensations).

### 3 Normative Sagas
1. **L2→L3 eviction:** upload segment → verify remote → retire local copy.
2. **Compaction publish:** write new segments → publish → update locators/manifests.
3. **Cross-region replication:** (implied) multi-step replication workflow with
   compensation on partial failure.

Sagas are deterministic and replayable: given same inputs, same sequence of steps
and compensations occurs, evidence recorded for debugging.

## Unit Test Specifications

### T1: remote_cap_required
Attempt remote operation without RemoteCap in Cx. Verify compile-time or runtime refusal.

### T2: remote_cap_enables_network
Provide RemoteCap in Cx. Verify remote operation proceeds (mock endpoint).

### T3: named_computation_serialization
Create each of the 4 normative computation names with inputs. Verify they serialize
to ComputationName + bytes, not closures.

### T4: idempotency_key_deterministic
Compute IdempotencyKey for same request_bytes twice. Verify identical Trunc128(BLAKE3) output.

### T5: idempotency_dedup_same_inputs
Submit same request twice with same IdempotencyKey. Verify second returns recorded outcome.

### T6: idempotency_conflict_different_inputs
Submit request with same IdempotencyKey but different computation inputs.
Verify rejection (conflict error).

### T7: lease_expiry_escalation
Set up remote handle with short lease. Let lease expire. Verify escalation
(cancel/retry/fail) and trace visibility.

### T8: saga_l2_l3_eviction_forward
Execute L2→L3 eviction saga forward path: upload → verify → retire.
Verify all steps complete and evidence recorded.

### T9: saga_l2_l3_eviction_compensation
Simulate failure at verify step of L2→L3 eviction. Verify compensation
rolls back upload and local state remains intact.

### T10: saga_compaction_publish_forward
Execute compaction publish saga: write segments → publish → update locators.
Verify complete and evidence recorded.

### T11: bulkhead_concurrency_cap
Set remote_max_in_flight=2. Launch 5 concurrent remote ops. Verify at most
2 are in flight simultaneously.

### T12: saga_deterministic_replay
Replay same saga with same inputs. Verify identical step sequence.

## Dependencies
- §3.5 (RaptorQ symbols), §4.17.1 (PRAGMA remote_max_in_flight), §4.15 (governor/bulkhead),
  §7.13 (compaction), §5.6.2 (leases)
","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:37:31Z"},{"id":210,"issue_id":"bd-numl","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Testing Requirements for §4.19.1 Remote Named Computations

### Unit Tests (fsqlite-core or fsqlite-remote crate)

**RemoteCap requirement:**
1. **test_remote_op_requires_remote_cap**: Without RemoteCap in Cx, remote operations refuse (compile-time or runtime). Verify no silent network I/O.
2. **test_lab_omits_remote_cap**: Lab contexts omit RemoteCap to test without network. Verify remote ops return appropriate error.
3. **test_native_mode_without_remote**: Under durability=local, system functions fully without RemoteCap.

**Named computations:**
4. **test_symbol_get_range_named**: `symbol_get_range(object_id, esi_lo, esi_hi, ecs_epoch)` is a named computation with serialized inputs.
5. **test_symbol_put_batch_named**: `symbol_put_batch(object_id, symbols[], ecs_epoch)` is named with serialized inputs.
6. **test_segment_put_named**: `segment_put(segment_id, bytes, ecs_epoch)` is named.
7. **test_segment_stat_named**: `segment_stat(segment_id, ecs_epoch)` is named.
8. **test_no_closure_shipping**: Verify runtime NEVER serializes arbitrary closures. Only ComputationName + serialized input bytes.

**Idempotency:**
9. **test_idempotency_key_derivation**: IdempotencyKey = Trunc128(BLAKE3("fsqlite:remote:v1" || request_bytes)). Verify correct computation.
10. **test_idempotency_dedup_same_request**: Same computation + same inputs → same IdempotencyKey → duplicate returns recorded outcome.
11. **test_idempotency_conflict_different_inputs**: Same IdempotencyKey but different computation inputs → conflict, MUST reject.
12. **test_idempotency_key_unique_per_request**: Different request bytes → different IdempotencyKeys (BLAKE3 collision resistance).

**Lease-backed liveness:**
13. **test_lease_expiry_escalates**: Remote handle lease expires → local region escalates (cancel, retry, or fail). Not a silent hang.
14. **test_lease_expiry_trace_visible**: Lease expiry event is trace-visible (appears in evidence ledger or logs).
15. **test_no_unbounded_remote_fetch**: Remote fetch that exceeds lease time is terminated. No "hung remote fetch" scenario.

**Sagas for multi-step publication:**
16. **test_saga_compensates_on_failure**: Multi-step remote+local workflow fails at step 3. Steps 1-2 are compensated (rolled back).
17. **test_saga_idempotent_compensation**: Compensation actions are themselves idempotent (safe to retry).
18. **test_saga_partial_state_impossible**: After saga completes (success or compensated failure), no partial state remains.

**Global remote bulkhead:**
19. **test_remote_bulkhead_concurrency_cap**: All remote ops run under global bulkhead. Concurrent ops capped at PRAGMA fsqlite.remote_max_in_flight.
20. **test_remote_bulkhead_zero_means_auto**: remote_max_in_flight=0 → auto-derived default.
21. **test_remote_bulkhead_prevents_retry_storms**: When remote tier degrades, bulkhead prevents runaway retry storms.

### Integration Tests
22. **test_remote_symbol_fetch_end_to_end**: Tiered storage scenario: fetch symbols from remote → decode → return page data.
23. **test_remote_saga_upload_compaction**: Multi-step: upload compacted segments → update locator → publish. Verify saga discipline.

### E2E Tests
24. **test_e2e_remote_failure_recovery**: Remote tier fails mid-operation. Verify saga compensation, bulkhead prevents cascade, local operations unaffected.

### Logging Requirements
- DEBUG: Named computation dispatch, IdempotencyKey derivation, lease renewal
- INFO: Remote operation completion (latency, computation name)
- WARN: Lease expiry, bulkhead saturation, idempotency conflict
- ERROR: Saga compensation failure (should be rare with idempotent compensators)

- Test validates MVCC snapshot isolation during concurrent read/write operations
- Reader threads see consistent snapshots unaffected by concurrent writers
- Writer commits become visible only to new transactions started after commit
- Tests at least 3 concurrent readers and 2 concurrent writers","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:18:53Z"},{"id":1042,"issue_id":"bd-oqi4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Task: Correctness test — MVCC isolation: snapshot reads during concurrent writes

### What
Demonstrate FrankenSQLite's MVCC advantage: readers see a consistent snapshot even while writers are actively modifying data. C SQLite's WAL mode allows this too, but with different characteristics.

### Test Design

**Phase 1: Writer thread**
- Begins a transaction
- Inserts 10,000 rows in batches of 100
- Commits after each batch
- Signals "done" when complete

**Phase 2: Reader thread (concurrent)**
- Opens a read transaction (BEGIN)
- Reads the table repeatedly during writer activity
- Verifies snapshot isolation:
  - Row count is consistent within a single read transaction
  - No partial batch visible (either all 100 rows from a batch or none)
  - No torn reads (half of a row's columns from old version, half from new)

**Phase 3: Verification**
- Writer completes all 10,000 rows
- Reader sees its snapshot (potentially stale — that's correct behavior)
- Reader closes transaction, re-reads — now sees all 10,000 rows
- Compare final state between backends

### MVCC-Specific Assertions
1. **Read stability**: Within a read transaction, repeated SELECTs return the same result (no phantom reads)
2. **No dirty reads**: Uncommitted writer changes are never visible to reader
3. **Snapshot freshness**: New read transaction after writer commits sees all changes

### Why This Is a Key Demo
This test directly demonstrates the MVCC advantage from §5 of the spec. C SQLite's WAL mode provides snapshot isolation for readers, but writers still serialize via WAL_WRITE_LOCK. FrankenSQLite's MVCC page-level versioning allows true concurrent readers and writers without the write serialization.

### Acceptance Criteria
- Snapshot isolation verified: reader sees consistent state throughout transaction
- No phantom reads within a read transaction
- No dirty reads
- Final state matches between backends after all transactions complete
- Test runs with both 1 writer + 1 reader and 1 writer + 4 readers

 {"id":"bd-ox09","title":"Corruption Injection + RaptorQ Repair Demo for SQLite DB Files","description":"## Goal
Create a dramatic, *real-file* durability demo:

1. Take a real SQLite DB (fixture).
2. Produce a correct mutated version (via workload harness).
3. Make an **encoded safety sidecar** (RaptorQ repair symbols + checksums).
4. Intentionally corrupt the DB copy (bit flips, truncation, torn-page simulation).
5. Show baseline `sqlite3` cannot self-heal (integrity check fails, data inaccessible).
6. Repair the corrupted DB using FrankenSQLite tooling that embodies “RaptorQ everywhere”.
7. Verify repaired DB matches the expected golden output (canonical sha256 match + integrity_check).

## Notes
- RaptorQ repairs *erasures*; for random corruption, we must detect corrupted chunks (checksums) and treat them as missing.
- Work at page/chunk granularity (default 4KB pages) to align with SQLite storage reality.

## Deliverables
- Corruption injector tool (safe: operates only on copies)
- Encoder tool producing sidecar symbols
- Repair tool that reconstructs missing/corrupted chunks
- End-to-end script that runs the full story and produces a report

## Acceptance

 {"id":"bd-q0oz","title":"§10.5 Query Planning: Cost Model, Index Selection, Join Ordering","description":"## SUMMARY

Implements the query planner: cost-model estimation, index selection, and join ordering. The cost model estimates access path costs primarily in page reads, using ANALYZE statistics (sqlite_stat1/sqlite_stat4) when available or heuristic fallbacks otherwise. Index usability rules determine which WHERE terms can exploit indexes. Join ordering uses bounded best-first beam search (NGQP-style, after C SQLite's wherePathSolver) with configurable mxChoice beam width, NOT exhaustive N! enumeration.

## KEY DATA STRUCTURES / ALGORITHMS

- **Cost model (page-read estimates)**:
  - Full table scan: `cost = N_pages(table)`
  - Index scan (range): `cost = log2(N_pages(index)) + selectivity * N_pages(index) + selectivity * N_pages(table)`
  - Index scan (equality): `cost = log2(N_pages(index)) + log2(N_pages(table))`
  - Covering index scan: `cost = log2(N_pages(index)) + selectivity * N_pages(index)`
  - Rowid lookup: `cost = log2(N_pages(table))`
- **Index usability rules**:
  - Equality (`col = expr`): usable if col is leftmost column of index.
  - Range (`col > expr`, `col BETWEEN`): usable as rightmost constraint.
  - IN (`col IN (...)`): expanded to multiple equality probes.
  - LIKE (`col LIKE 'prefix%'`): usable if prefix is constant.
- **Join ordering (beam search)**:
  - Maintain up to `mxChoice` best partial join paths at each level (lowest estimated cost).
  - `mxChoice` values: 1 (single-table), 5 (two-table), 12 or 18 (3+ tables; star-query heuristic may raise to 18).
  - Worst-case complexity: `O(mxChoice * N^2)` candidate expansions (bounded beam, not N!).
  - Based on C SQLite's `computeMxChoice` and `wherePathSolver()` in where.c.
- **Statistics sources**: `sqlite_stat1` (row counts, index selectivity), `sqlite_stat4` (distribution samples). Fallback: heuristic estimates when ANALYZE has not been run.

## NORMATIVE INVARIANTS

1. Cost model formulas use page reads as the primary cost unit (simplified for V1; C SQLite is more nuanced with CPU cost estimates).
2. Index equality usability requires the column to be the LEFTMOST column of the index.
3. Range constraints are usable only as the RIGHTMOST constraint in an index scan.
4. Join ordering uses bounded beam search, NOT exhaustive N! permutation.
5. mxChoice beam width is derived from join complexity (1/5/12/18), not a fixed constant.
6. There is no exhaustive N! search path -- beam search is the V1 strategy.

## UNIT TEST REQUIREMENTS

1. `test_cost_full_table_scan` -- Cost estimate for full scan equals N_pages(table); verify for tables of 1, 100, 10000 pages.
2. `test_cost_index_scan_range` -- Range scan cost includes log2(index pages) + selectivity * index pages + selectivity * table pages.
3. `test_cost_index_scan_equality` -- Equality scan cost is log2(index pages) + log2(table pages).
4. `test_cost_covering_index` -- Covering index scan omits table page lookups.
5. `test_cost_rowid_lookup` -- Rowid lookup cost is log2(table pages).
6. `test_index_usability_equality_leftmost` -- Only leftmost index column usable for equality.
7. `test_index_usability_range_rightmost` -- Range constraint usable only as rightmost constraint.
8. `test_index_usability_in_expansion` -- IN clause expands to multiple equality probes.
9. `test_index_usability_like_prefix` -- `LIKE 'abc%'` usable; `LIKE '%abc'` not usable.
10. `test_join_ordering_two_tables` -- Two-table join uses mxChoice=5; picks lower-cost order.
11. `test_join_ordering_three_tables` -- Three-table join uses mxChoice=12; beam search prunes suboptimal paths.
12. `test_join_ordering_star_query` -- Star-query heuristic raises mxChoice to 18 for 3+ table star joins.
13. `test_planner_uses_sqlite_stat1` -- When sqlite_stat1 exists, planner uses actual row counts instead of heuristics.
14. `test_planner_heuristic_fallback` -- Without ANALYZE, planner uses heuristic estimates and still produces valid plans.
15. `test_planner_selects_covering_index` -- When a covering index exists, planner prefers it over non-covering index + table lookup.

## E2E TEST

Create a multi-table schema with indexes and populated sqlite_stat1. Run `EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN` for various queries (single-table scan, indexed lookup, two-table join, three-table star join). Verify planner output matches expected access paths. Compare plan choices with C SQLite's EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN output for the same schema and data.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA

- Cost model correctly estimates page-read costs for all five access path types.
- Index usability rules correctly identify which WHERE terms can use which indexes.
- Beam search join ordering produces optimal or near-optimal plans for 2-table and 3+ table joins.
- mxChoice scales correctly with join complexity (1/5/12/18).
- ANALYZE statistics are consumed when available; heuristic fallback works without them.
- EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN output is human-readable and shows chosen access paths.
- No N! blowup: 10-table joins complete in bounded time.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:03:25.647860439Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T23:07:53.528363488Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T23:07:53.528324735Z","close_reason":"Implemented cost model (5 access path types), index usability analysis (equality/range/IN/LIKE prefix), and bounded beam search join ordering (mxChoice scaling 1/5/12/18, star-query heuristic, CROSS JOIN enforcement). 34 new tests, 56 total in planner crate. Clippy clean.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-q0oz","depends_on_id":"bd-18zh","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:03:26.708836623Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-q0oz","depends_on_id":"bd-1ik","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:09:56.051002935Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":121,"issue_id":"bd-q0oz","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Query Planning: Cost Model, Index Selection, Join Ordering

### Spec Content (Lines 13456-13498, section 10.5)

**10.5 Query Planning (lines 13456-13498)**

**Cost model** -- estimates cost in page reads:

| Access Path | Cost Formula |
|-------------|-------------|
| Full table scan | `N_pages(table)` |
| Index scan (range) | `log2(N_pages(index)) + selectivity * N_pages(index) + selectivity * N_pages(table)` |
| Index scan (equality) | `log2(N_pages(index)) + log2(N_pages(table))` |
| Covering index scan | `log2(N_pages(index)) + selectivity * N_pages(index)` |
| Rowid lookup | `log2(N_pages(table))` |

Note: These are simplified cost formulas for initial implementation. C SQLite's cost model is more nuanced with CPU cost estimates and per-row lookup cost for non-covering index scans.

When `ANALYZE` statistics are available (`sqlite_stat1`, `sqlite_stat4`), the planner uses actual row counts and distribution data; otherwise falls back to heuristic estimates.

**Index usability** -- for each WHERE term, determine if an index can satisfy it:
- **Equality** (`col = expr`): usable if `col` is the LEFTMOST column of an index
- **Range** (`col > expr`, `col BETWEEN`): usable as the RIGHTMOST constraint
- **IN** (`col IN (...)`): usable, expanded to multiple equality probes
- **LIKE** (`col LIKE 'prefix%'`): usable if prefix is constant

**Join ordering** -- bounded best-first search (beam search) in the style of C SQLite's NGQP (`wherePathSolver()` in `where.c`):
- Maintain up to `mxChoice` best partial join paths at each level (lowest estimated cost)
- `mxChoice` tuning knob derived from join complexity:
  - 1 for single-table
  - 5 for two-table
  - 12 or 18 for 3+ tables (star-query heuristic may raise to 18; see `computeMxChoice` in SQLite's `where.c`)
- Complexity: worst-case ~`O(mxChoice * N^2)` candidate expansions (bounded beam, NOT `N!`)
- This is the V1 strategy (no exhaustive N! search path)

### Unit Tests Required

1. **test_cost_full_table_scan**: For a table with 100 pages, verify cost estimate is 100.
2. **test_cost_rowid_lookup**: For a table with 1024 pages, verify cost is `log2(1024) = 10`.
3. **test_cost_index_scan_equality**: For index with 50 pages and table with 200 pages, verify cost is `log2(50) + log2(200)`.
4. **test_cost_index_scan_range**: For index with 50 pages, table with 200 pages, selectivity 0.1, verify cost is `log2(50) + 0.1 * 50 + 0.1 * 200 = ~30.6`.
5. **test_cost_covering_index_scan**: For index with 50 pages, selectivity 0.1, verify cost is `log2(50) + 0.1 * 50 = ~10.6` (no table lookup).
6. **test_cost_comparison_table_scan_vs_index**: Verify that for low selectivity, index scan cost < full table scan cost; for high selectivity (approaching 1.0), full table scan may be cheaper.
7. **test_index_usability_equality_leftmost**: Verify index on `(a, b, c)` is usable for `WHERE a = 1` but NOT for `WHERE b = 1` alone.
8. **test_index_usability_range_rightmost**: Verify index on `(a, b)` is usable for `WHERE a = 1 AND b > 5` (equality on a, range on b), but range on a with equality on b is less efficient.
9. **test_index_usability_in_clause**: Verify `WHERE col IN (1, 2, 3)` is recognized as usable for an index on `col`, expanded to 3 equality probes.
10. **test_index_usability_like_prefix**: Verify `WHERE name LIKE 'Jo%'` is usable for index on `name` (constant prefix), but `WHERE name LIKE '%Jo%'` is NOT usable (no constant prefix).
11. **test_join_ordering_single_table**: Verify `mxChoice = 1` for single-table query.
12. **test_join_ordering_two_tables**: Verify `mxChoice = 5` for two-table join and that the planner explores both orderings (t1, t2) and (t2, t1).
13. **test_join_ordering_three_tables**: Verify `mxChoice >= 12` for three-table join and that beam search prunes high-cost partial paths.
14. **test_join_ordering_prefers_cheaper_first**: For `t1 JOIN t2 ON t1.id = t2.fk` where t1 has 10 pages and t2 has 1000 pages with index on `fk`, verify planner chooses t1 first (smaller table drives the join).
15. **test_join_ordering_beam_search_bounded**: For a 6-table join, verify the planner does NOT explore all 720 (6!) orderings but uses bounded beam search.
16. **test_analyze_stats_override_heuristics**: Provide sqlite_stat1 data with actual row counts, verify cost estimates use those instead of heuristic estimates.
17. **test_cross_join_no_reorder**: Verify `CROSS JOIN` prevents the optimizer from reordering that join pair.

### E2E Tests

**test_e2e_explain_query_plan**: Execute `EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN SELECT * FROM t1 JOIN t2 ON t1.id = t2.fk WHERE t1.x > 10` on a database with indexes. Verify the plan shows the expected access path (index scan vs table scan) and join order.

**test_e2e_index_selection_with_statistics**: Create a table with 10000 rows, run `ANALYZE`, then execute a query with a selective WHERE clause. Verify (via EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN) that the planner chose the index path. Drop the index and verify the plan switches to table scan.

**test_e2e_join_ordering_cost_driven**: Create tables of different sizes with indexes. Execute a multi-table join and verify (via EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN) that the planner chooses the cost-optimal join order based on table sizes and available indexes.

### ScalarFunction (Send + Sync)
Equivalent to xFunc. Shared across connections, called concurrently. Methods: invoke(args: &[SqliteValue])->Result<SqliteValue>, is_deterministic()->bool (default true), num_args()->i32 (-1=variadic), name()->&str. Errors: FrankenError::Error for domain errors, FrankenError::TooBig for SQLITE_MAX_LENGTH.

### AggregateFunction (Send + Sync)
Equivalent to xStep + xFinal. Associated type State: Send. Factory method initial_state()->State (replaces Default bound since Box<dyn Any + Send> doesn't impl Default). Type erasure: FunctionRegistry stores Arc<dyn AggregateFunction<State = Box<dyn Any + Send>>>. Concrete impls use AggregateAdapter<F> wrapper. Methods: initial_state()->State, step(state, args), finalize(state)->SqliteValue, num_args, name.

### WindowFunction (Send + Sync)
Equivalent to xStep + xInverse + xValue + xFinal. Same State pattern as AggregateFunction. Key difference: inverse(state, args) for efficient row removal from sliding window frame. Methods: initial_state, step, inverse, value(state)->(non-consuming, current row result), finalize(state)->(consuming, final value), num_args, name.

## §9.3 Extension Traits

### VirtualTable (Send + Sync)
Equivalent to sqlite3_module. Associated type Cursor: VirtualTableCursor. Methods: create(db, args) (CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE, may create backing storage; default delegates to connect for eponymous), connect(db, args) (subsequent opens), best_index(info) (planner hints), open()->Cursor, disconnect(), destroy() (DROP, default delegates to disconnect), update(args)->Option<i64> (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE; default returns ReadOnly), begin/sync/commit/rollback, rename, savepoint/release/rollback_to.

### VirtualTableCursor (Send)
Methods: filter(idx_num, idx_str, args) (begin scan with planner params), next(), eof()->bool, column(ctx, col), rowid()->i64.

## §9.4 Collation and Authorization Traits

### CollationFunction (Send + Sync)
Equivalent to sqlite3_create_collation. Determines sort order. Built-in: BINARY (memcmp), NOCASE (case-insensitive ASCII), RTRIM (ignore trailing spaces). Methods: compare(a: &[u8], b: &[u8])->Ordering (deterministic, antisymmetric, transitive), name()->&str.

### Authorizer (Send + Sync)
Equivalent to sqlite3_set_authorizer. Called during compilation (not execution) to approve/deny operations. For sandboxing untrusted SQL. Methods: authorize(action, arg1, arg2, db_name, trigger)->AuthResult(Ok|Deny|Ignore).

AuthAction enum: CreateIndex, CreateTable, CreateTempIndex/Table/Trigger/View, CreateTrigger/View, Delete, DropIndex/Table/TempIndex/TempTable/TempTrigger/TempView/Trigger/View, Insert, Pragma, Read, Select, Transaction, Update, Attach, Detach, AlterTable, Reindex, Analyze, CreateVtable, DropVtable, Function, Savepoint, Recursive.

## §9.5 FunctionRegistry

Stores scalar/aggregate/window functions. Lookup by (name, arg_count) — case-insensitive (stored uppercase). Falls back to variadic (arg_count=-1) if exact match not found.

```rust
struct FunctionKey { name: String, num_args: i32 }
struct FunctionRegistry {
    scalars: HashMap<FunctionKey, Arc<dyn ScalarFunction>>,
    aggregates: HashMap<FunctionKey, Arc<dyn AggregateFunction<State = Box<dyn Any + Send>>>>,
    windows: HashMap<FunctionKey, Arc<dyn WindowFunction<State = Box<dyn Any + Send>>>>,
}
```

Methods: register_scalar, register_aggregate, register_window (overwrite existing same name+args), find_scalar, find_aggregate, find_window (return None if not found).

## §9.6 Trait Composition: How Layers Connect

Vfs+VfsFile -> Pager (owns Box<dyn VfsFile> for db file)
Pager+Wal -> MvccPager (wraps both; get_page checks version store -> Pager -> WAL via WalIndex -> db file)
MvccPager -> BtCursor (cursor calls pager.get_page during traversal, all through MVCC)
BtCursor -> VdbeCursor -> VDBE (OpenRead creates VdbeCursors wrapping BtCursors; Column extracts fields)
VDBE + FunctionRegistry -> Execution (Function/PureFunc opcodes lookup and call invoke/step/finalize)

## §9.7 Mock Implementations

Each trait has a mock for unit testing:
- MockVfs/MockVfsFile: Records calls, configurable responses. Pager tests simulate I/O errors.
- MockMvccPager: Pre-configured page data for (pgno, txn_id). B-tree tests isolate from MVCC.
- MockBtreeCursor: Pre-configured rows. VDBE tests.
- MockScalarFunction: Fixed return value. Codegen tests.

For sealed traits, mocks MUST live in defining crate (private sealed supertrait). Other crates use exported mock types/values.

 {"id":"bd-x1ww","title":"§4.3.1 E-Process Framework: Ville's Inequality + Betting Martingales","description":"Implement the core e-process statistical monitoring engine for FrankenSQLite's MVCC invariants (§4.3, spec lines ~3997-4130).

SCOPE AND PURPOSE: E-processes based on Ville's inequality provide anytime-valid statistical monitoring — you can peek at any time during execution without inflating type-I error, unlike classical hypothesis testing. Under the null hypothesis H0 (invariant holds, violation probability <= p0), the e-process is a non-negative supermartingale starting at 1. Rejection occurs when E_t >= 1/alpha at any stopping time, with guaranteed type-I error control.

KEY DATA STRUCTURES AND APIs:
- EProcessConfig: {p0: f64, lambda: f64, alpha: f64, max_evalue: f64} — per-invariant calibration parameters.

- Lambda constraint: lambda in (-1/(1-p0), 1/p0) for non-negativity.
- Mixture e-process: E_mix(t) = sum_j w_j * E_{lambda_j}(t), computed in log-space (log-sum-exp) for numerical stability.

**Spec Reference:** COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENSQLITE_V1.md §4.3, lines 3997–4130

## Overview

Implement the core e-process engine that provides anytime-valid statistical monitoring
for FrankenSQLite's MVCC invariants. E-processes (based on Ville's inequality) allow
peeking at any time during execution without inflating type-I error — unlike classical
hypothesis testing, no correction for multiple testing over time is needed.

## Formal Definition

An **e-process** `(E_t)_{t >= 0}` is a sequence of random variables adapted to a
filtration `(F_t)` such that:

1. `E_0 = 1` (starts at one)
2. `E_t >= 0` for all `t` (non-negative)
3. `E[E_t | F_{t-1}] <= E_{t-1}` (supermartingale under the null hypothesis H_0)

The null hypothesis H_0 asserts that the invariant holds (violation probability is
at most `p_0`, typically 0.001).

**Ville's inequality (key property):** For any stopping time `tau` and significance
level `alpha`:

```
P_{H_0}(exists t : E_t >= 1/alpha) <= alpha
```

This means you can peek at any time and reject H_0 (conclude the invariant is
systematically violated) if `E_t >= 1/alpha`, without inflating the type-I error rate.

## Fixed-λ Betting Martingale

The betting martingale update rule:

```
E_t = E_{t-1} * (1 + lambda * (X_t - p_0))
```

where:
- `lambda` is the bet size, constrained to `(-1/(1-p_0), 1/p_0)` for non-negativity
- `X_t` is the observation (1 = violation, 0 = no violation)
- `p_0` is the null hypothesis violation rate

Under H_0, `E[X_t] = p_0`, so `E[E_t | E_{t-1}] = E_{t-1}` (martingale).
Under the alternative H_1 (actual violation rate `p_1 > p_0`), the e-process grows
exponentially at rate `KL(p_1 || p_0)` per observation.

## Mixture E-Processes (Recommended Alien-Artifact Upgrade)

Fixed-λ is valid but brittle. Any nonnegative mixture of valid e-processes is itself
a valid e-process (by linearity of expectation):

```
E_mix(t) := Σ_j w_j * E_{λ_j}(t),   w_j >= 0, Σ_j w_j = 1
```

where each `E_{λ_j}` updates as `E_t = E_{t-1} * (1 + λ_j (X_t - p0))`.

### Practical Implementation (Normative):
- Choose `λ_j` on a **log grid** spanning "sensitive to rare violations" through
  "sensitive to frequent violations" (e.g., **16–64 values**)
- Maintain `log(E_{λ_j})` and compute the mixture in **log-space** (log-sum-exp)
  for numerical stability
- Alarm when `E_mix(t) >= 1/alpha` (same Ville guarantee; optional stopping safe)

This gives near-oracle power across a wide range of `p_1` without per-invariant
hand-tuning, while preserving the same statistical guarantee under H0.

## Alpha Budget (Union Bound) for Multiple Monitors

FrankenSQLite runs many monitors (INV-1..INV-7, INV-SSI-FP, symbol survival,
replication divergence, etc.). Two approaches for family-wise error control:

1. **Alpha budget (union bound, simplest):** choose per-monitor levels `alpha_i`
   such that `sum_i alpha_i <= alpha_total`. Each monitor rejects when
   `E_i(t) >= 1/alpha_i`.

2. **E-value aggregation (adaptive, recommended):** choose weights `w_i >= 0`
   with `sum_i w_i = 1` and define:

   ```
   E_global(t) := Σ_i w_i * E_i(t)
   ```

   This is a valid e-process under the global null **regardless of dependence**
   between monitors (Vovk & Wang 2021, §4). Critical because MVCC invariant
   monitors observe the same transactions and are therefore correlated.

   The resulting certificate includes top contributing monitors by `w_i * E_i(t)`
   share (an "evidence ledger").

   *Note:* Weighted geometric mean `Π_i E_i(t)^{w_i}` would be tighter but
   requires conditional independence (which does not hold here). Arithmetic mean
   is the standard dependence-robust aggregation.

## Implementation Structs

```rust
use asupersync::lab::oracle::eprocess::{EProcess, EProcessConfig};

pub struct EProcessConfig {
    pub p0: f64,        // Null hypothesis violation rate
    pub lambda: f64,    // Bet size (fixed-λ) or unused if mixture
    pub alpha: f64,     // Significance level
    pub max_evalue: f64, // Cap to prevent f64 overflow
}

pub struct EProcess {
    pub name: &'static str,
    pub config: EProcessConfig,
    pub e_value: f64,          // Current e-value (starts at 1.0)
    pub observations: u64,     // Count of observations
    pub rejected: bool,        // Whether H0 has been rejected
}

pub struct MixtureEProcess {
    pub name: &'static str,
    pub components: Vec<(f64, EProcess)>,  // (weight, component)
    pub alpha: f64,
    pub log_components: Vec<f64>,          // log-space for stability
}
```

## Expected Detection Delay Formula

For a monitor with `p0` and `lambda`, the expected detection delay when true
violation rate is `p1`:

```
N_detect ≈ log(1/alpha) / KL(p1 || p0)
```

where `KL(p1 || p0) = p1 * ln(p1/p0) + (1-p1) * ln((1-p1)/(1-p0))`.

## Unit Test Specifications

### Test 1: `test_eprocess_initial_state`
Verify that a newly created EProcess has `e_value == 1.0`, `observations == 0`,
and `rejected == false`.

### Test 2: `test_eprocess_no_violations_stays_near_one`
Feed 10,000 `X_t = 0` (no violation) observations to an EProcess with
`p0 = 0.001, lambda = 0.9`. Assert `e_value` stays within `[0.0, 2.0]`
(it should decay slightly under pure null, since each step multiplies by
`(1 - lambda * p0)`).

### Test 3: `test_eprocess_single_violation_jump`
With `lambda = 0.999, p0 = 1e-9`, observe one violation (`X_t = 1`).
The e-value should jump by approximately `(1 + lambda * (1 - p0)) ≈ 2.0`.
Assert `e_value` is in `[1.9, 2.1]`.

### Test 4: `test_eprocess_rejects_on_systematic_violations`
Feed violations at rate `p1 = 0.01` (10x the null rate of `p0 = 0.001`) with
`lambda = 0.5, alpha = 0.05`. After enough observations, `rejected` must be
`true`. Expected detection delay: `log(20) / KL(0.01, 0.001) ≈ 430` observations.
Feed 2000 and assert rejection occurred.

### Test 5: `test_mixture_eprocess_valid`
Create a mixture of 16 components on a log grid of λ values from 0.01 to 0.99.
Feed 5000 observations with no violations. Assert `E_mix` stays below `1/alpha`.
Then feed violations at rate 0.05 and assert rejection occurs within 500 observations.

### Test 6: `test_lambda_constraint_enforced`
Attempt to create an EProcess with `lambda` outside `(-1/(1-p0), 1/p0)`.
Assert that creation panics or returns an error.

### Test 7: `test_log_space_stability`
Create a mixture e-process and feed 100,000 observations alternating between
violation and no-violation. Assert no NaN, no infinity, and no negative e-values
at any point during the sequence.

### Test 8: `test_alpha_budget_union_bound`
Create 7 monitors with `alpha_i` such that `sum(alpha_i) = 0.01`. Under the
global null (no real violations in any monitor), run 100,000 observations each.
Assert that no monitor rejects (with overwhelming probability).

### Test 9: `test_evalue_aggregation_rejects_correlated`
Create 4 monitors with equal weights `w_i = 0.25`. Feed correlated observations
(all monitors see the same violations). Assert that `E_global` rejects and the
evidence ledger correctly identifies the contributing monitors.

### Test 10: `test_max_evalue_cap`
With `max_evalue = 1e18`, feed enough violations to drive `e_value` past the cap.
Assert it is clamped to `max_evalue` and does not overflow to infinity.

## Purpose

This sub-epic ensures that Phase 5 wiring doesn't break existing functionality and that
the new storage stack works correctly end-to-end. It provides:

1. **Regression guard** — existing 5,319 tests must still pass after each phase
2. **End-to-end validation** — comprehensive test script exercising the full pipeline
3. **C SQLite compatibility** — .db files must be readable by both implementations
4. **Crash recovery** — WAL replay must produce consistent state after simulated crash

## Why this is critical

Phase 5 changes the fundamental data flow of the system. Without rigorous testing:
- Subtle data corruption could go unnoticed until production use
- Performance regressions could mask correctness issues
- WAL/pager interactions may have edge cases only visible under load
- C SQLite compatibility is essential for migration and interop

## Logging philosophy

All tests in Phase 5F use **structured logging** (not just println/eprintln):
- Log format: `[PHASE][TEST_NAME][STEP] message | key1=value1 key2=value2`
- Timing: every significant operation is timed with `Instant::now()` / `elapsed()`
- Assertions log expected vs actual before asserting
- On failure: dump full database state (page count, WAL size, btree structure)
- Test harness captures logs to file for post-mortem analysis

## Children

- bd-16e7: 5F.1 — Per-phase regression guard
- bd-jd39: 5F.2 — End-to-end storage stack test script
- bd-3r48: 5F.3 — C SQLite file format compatibility
- bd-3tc7: 5F.4 — Crash recovery and WAL replay test
